observations.managementAction: The Inventory Committee already communicated to all departments for the coordination and assisting the inventory committee in conducting the physical inventory. The Inventory Committee conducted a physical count of PPEs but the activity was only limited to office-based equipment. Additionally, the Physical Inventory Report was submitted beyond the prescribed period.
observations.reason:
No inventory records or related documents were turned over to the Acting GSO upon his designation in CY 2024.
CIPLC and RPLC are yet to be submitted by the Municipal Accountant.
An inventory of all roads in the Municipality as of January 31, 2024 was submitted to the Municipal Accountant; however, it was not prepared using the prescribed template, resulting in the omission of several pertinent details.
The Municipal Assessor assisted management in securing titles for several parcels of land owned by the Municipality. As of this writing, only two lots remain untitled, one of which is already registered and currently being processed by the Registry of Deeds.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the management agreed:
i. to prioritize the filling of the mandatory position (i.e., administration and training position) required by R.A. No. 10121 and NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC JMC No. 2014-1 for the effective, efficient and continued discharge of the duties and responsibilities of the LDRRMO;
ii. that the MDRRMO Head ensure the preparation and submission of the Report on Utilization of DRRM Funds, which must comprehensively reflect all sources of funds and their corresponding utilization in accordance with COA Circular 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012; and
iii. that the Municipal Accountant include the appropriate Note to the Financial Statements in compliance with Section 2, Item VII of COA Circular No. 2014-002 dated April 15, 2014, to properly disclose the receipt, distribution, and status of relief goods, thereby informing users of the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality, through the efforts of the MDRRMO, was awarded the “Gawad Kalasag Seal of Excellence” conferred by the National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council on December 13, 2024 with a “Beyond Compliant” rating. Management abolished one high-salary-grade position in the MDRRMO and created two lower-salary-grade positions in its place, one of which has already been filled in CY 2024. The remaining vacant position will be evaluated for possible filling once revenue collections improve. The MDRRMO Head will ensure complete detail of the source of funds and their utilization will be submitted moving forward. With regard to donations, Management will ensure that the necessary resolutions are secured from the Sangguniang Bayan to formally acknowledge their receipt and will furnish copies to the concerned offices for their information and appropriate action.
observations.reason: The position designated for 'Operations and Warning' remains vacant; Several fund receipts were omitted from the DRRMF report due to the MDRRMO Head's lack of awareness that such funds must be included; Non-disclosure of in-kind donations in the Notes to Financial Statements occurred because information was not communicated to the Municipal Accountant.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the management agreed:
i. that the concerned department heads secure the necessary Memoranda of Agreement or Terms of Agreement for all funds received from source agencies to ensure clear accountability and prevent any potential misinterpretation or misuse of the transferred funds;
ii. that the focal person of the Senior Citizen’s Office post the final list of beneficiaries to promote transparency and accountability in the selection and distribution process; and
iii. to work collectively towards ensuring the duration and timely completion of projects, thus enabling the prompt realization of benefits for the intended beneficiaries.
observations.managementAction: Management is already coordinating with the source agency for the MOA on the fund transfer. The Municipal Accountant and/or Treasurer ensure compliance with the posting and reportorial requirements of each trust fund to maintain transparency and accountability in the management of financial resources sourced externally. The Acting Municipal Engineer closely monitored the project during implementation to ensure its quality; and carried out regular inspection after the completion to avoid any additional repair or reconstruction cost.
observations.reason: Some source agencies did not provide MOAs/Terms of Agreement; The final list of SPISC beneficiaries was not posted in conspicuous places as required; Delay in project completion occurred (FMR Sitio Lacmit delayed by 28 calendar days) and formal documentation for extensions/suspensions were not properly issued.

observations.recommendation:
1.20. We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed that the:
a. MENRO Head (i) regularly coordinate with the GSO/Supply Officer
and Municipal Accountant, on a monthly basis, regarding the details of
received and expected incentives, to ensure timely and accurate recognition
and disclosure in the books of accounts of the Municipality; and (ii) actively
monitor and follow up on the details of incentives earned by the Municipality,
ensuring that these are secured and timely recorded within the period they
were earned;
b. GSO/Supply Officer and Municipal Accountant, prepare and maintain
all the stock cards, ledger cards, SSMI and other related reports in the receipt
and issuance of inventories; and
c. Municipal Accountant draw a journal entry to recognize the inventory
valued at ₱302,940.00 and, henceforth, comply with the required recognition
in the books of inventories resulting from the partnerships of the municipality.

2.29. We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the:
a. Inventory Committee to (i) conduct a complete physical inventory and
submit the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and
Equipment to the office of the Audit Team not later than January 31 of
each year; and (ii) ensure the proper tagging of all properties and adopt a
standardized system of property numbering;
b. GSO-designate to (i) maintain Property Cards based on source
documents, such as Purchase Orders, Delivery Receipts/Invoices, and
Inspection and Acceptance Reports, ensuring that all required details are
accurately and completely recorded for each type of property; and (ii)
maintain Local Road Inventory and Road Map and Local Road Network
Property Card;
c. Municipal Accountant to (i) prepare and maintain Property, Plant and
Equipment Ledger Card (PPELC), Construction-in-Progress Ledger
Cards (CIPLC) and Real Property Ledger Cards (RPLC); and (ii) draw
the necessary journal entry voucher/s to reflect the adjustments of
misclassified PPEs;
d. Municipal Engineering Office to provide the Municipal Accountant and
the GSO-designate the complete description and cost segregation of road
components for road projects using the prescribed format as basis of the
Municipal Accountant to recognize the same in the books of accounts and
the GSO-designate to complete his property records; and
e. Municipal Assessor to fast track the necessary actions for the registration
and titling of the remaining landholdings.

3.37. We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the:
(a) Municipal Engineer, being the building official, to inform all applicants
of building permits of their responsibilities after the completion of the
structure, which include, among others, the filing of a sworn declaration of
the true value of the structure. Likewise, inspect properties applied for
building permits that have not yet been applied for an occupancy permit and
compel the owner to perform their duties mandated by the law;
(b) Municipal Assessor to verify the status of undeclared property and
henceforth declare the same in the name of the defaulting owner, if known,
or against an unknown owner, as the case may be, and assess the property for
taxation in accordance with laws, rules and regulations;
(c) Municipal Treasurer to (i) regularly prepare and issue the necessary
notice of delinquency and demand letters to delinquent taxpayers; and (ii)
post the notice of the delinquency at the main entrance hall and in a publicly
accessible and conspicuous place in each barangay of the municipality where
the property is located; and
(d) Municipal Accountant to draw a journal entry voucher to recognize the
correcting entries on the understated property tax receivable accounts.

4.40. We recommended and Local Chief Executive agreed that:
a. The Municipal Treasurer
i. Prioritize the finalization of the stratified schedule of presumptive
income level based on industry classifications, business size, location,
and other relevant operational indicators to approximate the gross
receipts of each business classification for every tax period. Once
established, it may serve as a standard reference in estimating gross
sales or receipts for businesses that fail to provide sufficient
documentation.
ii. Require all persons, who are granted a permit to conduct an activity or
business and who are liable, to pay the business tax to submit a certified
photocopy of their income tax returns (ITR) on or before April 30 of
each year and collect any tax deficiency, if any, including interest at the
rate of ten percent (10%) corresponding to the two percent (2%) per
month from January to May. Otherwise, enforce the corresponding
penalty for non-compliance therewith;
iii. Conduct a thorough investigation into the underlying causes of the
decline in the number of business permits and license renewals,
including potential cases of unreported business closures and enjoin
business establishments to settle local taxes, fees, and charges prior to
the formal retirement or closure of their operations;
iv. Collect the tax deficiency from the concerned business entities; and
v. Enforce other remedies to ensure that the correct amount of fees and
taxes are collected.
b. The Business Permit and Licensing Officer ensure that applicants (i)
submit a complete set of all documentary requirements in securing
permits and licenses prior to the approval of the Local Chief Executive;
and (ii) declare the correct amount of gross sales in their application
consistent with the provisions of the Revenue Ordinance.

5.16. We recommended and the management agreed to (a) discontinue the practice
of lump-sum budgeting and the inclusion of generic items in the Annual Investment
Plan and Annual Budget, otherwise obtain prior authorization from the Local
Sanggunian and proper documentation to support the availability of funds for PPAs
to be charged from lumpsum appropriation; and (b) strengthen the planning process
by substantiating project proposals with detailed specifications and cost estimates in
compliance with Section 3.2.3 of the JMC.

6.26. We recommended and the management agreed:
i. to prioritize the filling of the mandatory position (i.e., administration and
training position) required by R.A. No. 10121 and NDRRMC-DILG-
DBM-CSC JMC No. 2014-1 for the effective, efficient and continued
discharge of the duties and responsibilities of the LDRRMO;
ii. that the MDRRMO Head ensure the preparation and submission of the
Report on Utilization of DRRM Funds, which must comprehensively
reflect all sources of funds and their corresponding utilization in
accordance with COA Circular 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012; and
iii. that the Municipal Accountant include the appropriate Note to the
Financial Statements in compliance with Section 2, Item VII of COA
Circular No. 2014-002 dated April 15, 2014, to properly disclose the
receipt, distribution, and status of relief goods, thereby informing users of
the financial statements.

7.24. We recommended and the management agreed:
i. that the concerned department heads secure the necessary Memoranda of
Agreement or Terms of Agreement for all funds received from source
agencies to ensure clear accountability and prevent any potential
misinterpretation or misuse of the transferred funds;
ii. that the focal person of the Senior Citizen’s Office post the final list of
beneficiaries to promote transparency and accountability in the selection
and distribution process; and
iii. to work collectively towards ensuring the duration and timely completion
of projects, thus enabling the prompt realization of benefits for the
intended beneficiaries.

8.42. We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the:
a. Chairman and Members of the BAC and TWG to (i) invite all necessary
observers in different stages of procurement; (ii) carefully scrutinize the
documentary requirements submitted by the bidder; (iii) require COSH
Program approved by DOLE for all infrastructure projects prior to
perfection of contract; (iv) diligently observe each procurement
activity stated in the RIRR of R.A. No. 9184; and (v) attend seminars and
trainings related to government procurement to keep abreast of pertinent
provisions thereof;
b. Municipal Accountant to (i) coordinate with the BAC and submit all the
documentary requirements, if warranted, otherwise submit an explanation
for non-submission thereof; (ii) apply the provisions governing the
retention money; and (iii) verify all supporting documents as to the
completeness and ensure that the same formed part of the supporting
documents to the disbursement vouchers before effecting payments
thereof; and
c. Acting - Municipal Engineer to issue formal orders for any contract time
extension, if found valid and in accordance with Item 11, Annex E, of the
RIRR of R.A. No. 9184; otherwise, impose liquidated damages for delays
incurred in project completion.

9.27. We recommended and the management agreed:
a. that the Municipal Accountant (i) require all employees, officials, and
disbursing officers granted cash advances to prepare and submit
liquidation reports or reports of disbursements, whichever is applicable, in
support of the liquidation of their cash advances; and (ii) ensure that no
additional cash advances are granted to any employee or officer unless all
previous advances have been fully liquidated and settled in accordance
with existing regulations; and
b. to ensure that the cash accountability of an accountable officer will not
exceed the maximum accountability covered by the approved bond.

10.11. We reiterated our recommendation and the management agreed to require all
COS personnel to submit accomplishment reports to support their payroll claims.
Furthermore, we recommended and the management agreed that future contracts for
COS personnel include a provision expressly requiring the submission of Daily Time
Records (DTRs) and accomplishment reports for every payroll period.

11.8. We recommended and the Municipal Planning and Development Officer,
together with the GAD Focal Person and TWG, agreed to prioritize the establishment
of a GAD database containing gender-disaggregated data across relevant sectors that
will serve as basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy
formulation.

12.28. We reiterated our recommendations and the Local Chief Executive agreed
that the MENRO Head and his subordinates:
(i) enjoin all barangays to support the solid waste management program of
the municipality through continued efforts to educate their respective
constituents on the proper segregation of reusable or recyclable materials
from compostable wastes; strict enforcement of “No Segregation, No
Collection Policy”; and establishment of functional MRF in all barangays
or clustered MRF among barangays;
(ii) stop accepting and accommodating wastes not considered residual from
the barangays; and
(iii) strengthen the implementation of existing ordinances and policies on solid
waste management especially on the violators of open burning.

12.29. Likewise, we recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed that
collectors and other personnel directly dealing with the collection of solid waste be
regularly equipped with personal protective equipment and proper training to
protect them from the hazards of handling solid waste.

13.8. However, for past due accounts based on the GSIS-EBCS, we reiterated our
recommendation and the Accounting Office agreed to consistently confer with the
GSIS until the discrepancies between the records of the Municipality and the GSIS
are resolved.

14.7. We commended the Management for the accurate withholding of premiums
and loan repayments from its personnel, in compliance with R.A. No. 9679 and its
IRR, as well as the timely remittance of these amounts, including government shares,
within the prescribed period outlined in HDMF Circular No. 275. Additionally, we
recognized their adherence to the new contribution rates under HDMF Circular No.
460. Nevertheless, we recommended and the Accounting Office agreed to continue to
ensure compliance with the requirements of these issuances.

15.7. We commended Management for its consistent adherence to existing laws,
rules, and regulations on the proper deduction and remittance of PhilHealth
contributions for its employees and personnel, in accordance with R.A. No. 10606, as
amended. Nevertheless, we recommended and the management agreed to continue the
observance of these provisions to sustain the benefits for its employees.

16.8. We commended the Management for its accurate withholding and prompt
remittance of taxes to the BIR. Nonetheless, we recommended and the management
agreed to continue strict adherence to withholding and timely remittance in line with
Revenue Memorandum Circular Nos. 23-007 and 23-2012, and BIR Revenue
Regulations No. 2-98.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the:
a. Municipal Treasurer to prepare RCI based on checks released to released to claimants and submit the same to the Municipal Accountant together with the disbursement vouchers and supporting documents; and
b. Municipal Accountant to (i) verify the accuracy of the report through examination of all disbursement vouchers; and (ii) revert to cash and respective liability accounts all unissued checks as of yearend.
observations.managementAction: RCI based on checks released to released to claimants is being prepared and submitted to the Municipal Accountant together with the disbursement vouchers and supporting documents; and together with the disbursement vouchers and supporting documents. The municipal accountant verified the RCI and all the submitted disbursement vouchers and liability accounts was established on the unissued checks as of year-end.

Compliance with the recommendation in the CY 2022 Annual Audit Report was partially achieved, as evidenced by the declaration for tax assessment and collection purposes of 207 properties completed in CYs 2018 to 2024. The Municipal Treasurer’s Office issued demand letters to delinquent owners of real properties. The Provincial Government of Pampanga (PGP) furnished the Municipal Treasurer with a copy of an abstract of real property taxes collected by the former showing, among others, the computation of the shares of concerned local government units. As of year-end, they have not yet received their shares from these collections; hence, it remains a receivable from the PGP. From available records of the MTO, PGP has collected RPT and SET in the aggregate amount of ₱272,152.94 in the last quarter of CY 2024. The Municipal Accountant stated that the recognition of property tax receivables has consistently relied on the information provided by the Municipal Treasurer. He also admitted that comparison between the two sets of records was not made, which resulted in the variance going undetected. Likewise, correcting entries were not drawn to reconcile the balances of RPT and SET accounts. Nevertheless, he committed to initiating corrective measures and ensuring the accuracy of recorded balances moving forward.
observations.reason: While declaration for tax assessment and collection purposes of properties was undertaken (207 properties), 66 completed properties with an estimated value of 298.934 million remained undeclared; Receivables from Provincial collections (₱272,152.94) were not recognized in the books; an understatement of RPT and SET receivables amounting to ₱1,203,404.00 was noted due to errors in recognition; Notice of Delinquencies was not posted in required public places as per Article 345, Rule XXXI of R.A. No. 7160.

observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer has already prepared a stratified schedule of presumptive income levels, which is currently pending legislation by the Sangguniang Bayan. All documentary requirements in securing permits and licenses prior to the approval of the Local Chief Executive are being required by the BPLO. The MTO reported that they have initiated the development of a stratified schedule of presumptive income levels. The Municipal Treasury Office was able to obtain and submit a copy of the CY 2023 ITR of 104 business entities in response to the Audit Team’s request. The BPLO and the Municipal Treasury Office issued demand letters to business entities that have not yet renewed their permits and licenses, in an effort to recover fees and taxes. Corrective action was taken by assessing and collecting deficiency taxes from entities during the business permit renewal process in the first quarter of CY 2025.
observations.reason: Stratified schedule pending legislation by Sangguniang Bayan; Not all taxpayers submitted ITRs (only 104 obtained); Acceptance of applications with incomplete documentary requirements persists; Some business entities did not settle taxes prior to retirement/closure; Under assessment and collection deficiencies noted in sample taxpayers which required reassessment and collection actions that were only undertaken in CY 2025.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the management agreed to (a) discontinue the practice of lump-sum budgeting and the inclusion of generic items in the Annual Investment Plan and Annual Budget, otherwise obtain prior authorization from the Local Sanggunian and proper documentation to support the availability of funds for PPAs to be charged from lumpsum appropriation; and (b) strengthen the planning process by substantiating project proposals with detailed specifications and cost estimates in compliance with Section 3.2.3 of the JMC.
observations.managementAction: No delays were noted in the implementation and completion of projects funded under 20% DF.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the:
a. Chairman and Members of the BAC and TWG to (i) invite all necessary observers in different stages of procurement; (ii) carefully scrutinize the documentary requirements submitted by the bidder; (iii) require COSH Program approved by DOLE for all infrastructure projects prior to perfection of contract; and (iv) diligently observe each procurement activity stated in the RIRR of R.A. No. 9184;
b. Municipal Accountant to (i) coordinate with the BAC and submit all the documentary requirements, if warranted, otherwise submit an explanation for non-submission thereof; and (ii) verify all supporting documents as to the completeness and ensure that the same formed part of the supporting documents to the disbursement vouchers before effecting payments thereof; and
c. Acting - Municipal Engineer to issue formal orders for any contract time extension, if found valid and in accordance with Item 11, Annex E, of the RIRR of R.A. No. 9184; otherwise, impose liquidated damages for delays incurred in project completion.
observations.managementAction: The BAC Secretariat explained that several of the observations were addressed only after the CY 2023 audit exit conference in May 2024. The Acting Municipal Engineer supervised the construction to ensure that the contractor adheres to the specifications. The Municipal Accountant coordinated with the BAC and submitted supporting documents where available.
observations.reason: Pre-procurement conferences not conducted for some projects; observers not invited in detailed evaluation and post-qualification stages; required supporting documents (SLCC supporting docs, Notices, Contracts, NTPs, end-user acceptance) missing for several projects; NFCC computations contained inaccuracies; COSH Program not submitted/approved prior to perfection of contract for several projects; Notice of Award issued beyond reglementary period for at least one project; liquidated damages not imposed despite project delays due to absence of formal approved time extensions. Other documentary requirements for payments (trip tickets, attendance records, pre/post inspection reports) were incomplete.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the management agreed:
a. that the Municipal Accountant (i) require all employees, officials, and disbursing officers granted cash advances to prepare and submit liquidation reports or reports of disbursements, whichever is applicable, in support of the liquidation of their cash advances; and (ii) ensure that no additional cash advances are granted to any employee or officer unless all previous advances have been fully liquidated and settled in accordance with existing regulations; and
b. to ensure that the cash accountability of an accountable officer will not exceed the maximum accountability covered by the approved bond.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant explained that he was only familiar with the use of Liquidation Reports (LRs) and will inform Accountable Officers to properly prepare and submit the necessary reports moving forward. Management responded that Department Heads regularly involved in programs and activities requiring cash advances have applied for their respective fidelity bonds. Premium amounts will be increased to adequately secure their accountabilities.
observations.reason: Liquidation Reports (LRs) were incorrectly submitted in place of Reports of Disbursements (RDs) for numerous cash advances; additional cash advances were granted despite existence of unliquidated advances for some AOs (overlapping advances observed); the fidelity bond of the MSWDO Head (special disbursing officer) was insufficient relative to the cash accountability during large disbursements such as Social Pension distributions, leaving excess accountability unbonded.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendations that management initiate the following actions:
a. adhere to the rules and regulations outlined for the hiring of Job Order personnel;
b. refrain from hiring Job Order personnel whose functions overlap with those regular employees or are deemed recurring, necessary, and essential to the Municipality; and
c. implement strict control measures to ensure timely submission of Daily Time Records (DTRs) before the actual payout date as basis for payroll preparation.
We further recommended that all COS personnel be required to submit accomplishment reports to support their payroll claims and that future contracts for COS personnel include provisions requiring submission of DTRs and accomplishment reports for every payroll period.
observations.managementAction: During the validation process that started on December 17, 2024, and continued through several extensions, all 195 job order and 11 COS personnel were successfully evaluated, verified, or appeared in person at the auditor’s office. Management committed to require COS personnel to submit Accomplishment Reports to support their payroll claims and to include clear provisions in future contracts requiring submission of DTRs and Accomplishment Reports for every payroll period.
observations.reason: COS personnel did not submit accomplishment reports for CY 2024; Municipality continues to renew JO and COS contracts every six months even where functions overlap with regular positions due to personnel limitations; strict control measures on DTR submission not fully enforced.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Municipal Planning and Development Officer, together with the GAD Focal Person and TWG, agreed to prioritize the establishment of a GAD database containing gender-disaggregated data across relevant sectors that will serve as basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation.
observations.managementAction: The GAD Focal Point Person disclosed that while they are waiting for the final output of the Community-Based Monitoring System (CBMS) field data collection led by the Philippine Statistics Office, the management continuously relied on historical data collected and used on previous years’ programming with the adoption of several updates, as needed, from key offices as foundation for its planning and actions. The GAD FP Person consistently coordinated with the MBO to ensure the accuracy of reports submitted to oversight agencies. The GAD FPS prioritized capacity building and conducted capacity development training in the last quarter of this year.
observations.reason: Establishment of the GAD database is still in process pending the final output of CBMS field data collection; reliance on historical data continues while GAD database is not yet operational.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendations and the Local Chief Executive agreed that the MENRO Head and his subordinates:
(i) enjoin all barangays to support the solid waste management program of the municipality through continued efforts to educate their respective constituents on the proper segregation of reusable or recyclable materials from compostable wastes; strict enforcement of “No Segregation, No Collection Policy”; and establishment of functional MRF in all barangays or clustered MRF among barangays;
(ii) stop accepting and accommodating wastes not considered residual from the barangays; and
(iii) strengthen the implementation of existing ordinances and policies on solid waste management especially on the violators of open burning.
Likewise, we recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed that collectors and other personnel directly dealing with the collection of solid waste be regularly equipped with personal protective equipment and proper training to protect them from the hazards of handling solid waste.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality sustained its collaboration with various private organizations in its ongoing efforts to address waste management challenges and tapped HOLCIM which provided training in handling of waste. The Municipality received incentives and savings from partnerships. The MENRO Head acknowledged issues and committed to corrective measures. Efforts were made to issue PPE (gloves, facemasks, safety boots) though ocular inspection revealed insufficiency. MENRO has conducted meetings with barangay personnel.
observations.reason: Despite partnerships and trainings (e.g., with HOLCIM), challenges remain in implementing segregation at source and establishing functional MRFs across barangays; some instances of open burning observed; PPE issuance to collectors is insufficient and not regularly maintained; 1,188 bags of cement incentives from Republic Cement in CY 2024 remained unrecorded due to absence of formal communication from MENRO Head.

observations.recommendation:
We commended the Municipal Accounting Office for adhering to the GSIS rules on the timely remittance of withheld premiums, government shares and loan amortization for CY 2024.
However, for past due accounts based on the GSIS-EBCS, we reiterated our recommendation and the Accounting Office agreed to consistently confer with the GSIS until the discrepancies between the records of the Municipality and the GSIS are resolved. We also recommended that the concerned official and/or employee settle or restructure their arrears.
observations.managementAction: The unpaid premiums have decreased by P185,563.26 (from P239,705.29 in CY 2023 to P54,142.03 in CY 2024) due to the continuous reconciliation by the Accounting Office with GSIS. The Management sent several letters to the incumbent vice mayor in the process of requiring him to settle or restructure the arrears. The Accounting Office continues to reconcile and correspond with GSIS, submitting GSIS Form C, employee service records, and copies of appointment papers as needed.
observations.reason: Discrepancies with GSIS eBCS still exist (aggregate unpaid premiums ₱54,142.03 as of December 31, 2024). One notable loan (incumbent vice mayor) remained unpaid as of December 31, 2024 despite communications to settle or restructure.

observations.recommendation: We commended the Management for the accurate withholding of premiums and loan repayments from its personnel, in compliance with R.A. No. 9679 and its IRR, and remitted the same in full together with the government shares to the Home Development Mutual Fund (HDMF) within the prescribed period outlined in HDMF Circular No. 275. We recommended and the Accounting Office agreed to continue to ensure compliance with the requirements of these issuances.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2024, the Municipality consistently complied with the required deductions and remittances of premium contributions and loan amortizations amounting to ₱1,333,781.25 from the compensation and wages of its regular or permanent employees, including personnel under Job Order (JO) and Contract of Service (COS) status, in compliance with the above provisions, leaving no outstanding balance at year-end. Management committed to continue observing the requirements and to increase premiums to obtain adequate fidelity bonding where needed.

b. Municipal Accountant to (i) coordinate with the BAC and submit all the
documentary requirements, if warranted, otherwise submit an
explanation for non-submission thereof; (ii) apply the provisions
governing the retention money; and (iii) verify all supporting documents
as to the completeness and ensure that the same formed part of the
supporting documents to the disbursement vouchers before effecting
payments thereof; and
c. Acting - Municipal Engineer to supervise all projects during the
construction period to ensure that the contractor adheres to
specifications, timelines and quality standards.

8.6 We reiterated our recommendations that management initiate the following
actions:
a. adhere to the rules and regulations outlined for the hiring of Job Order
personnel;
b. refrain from hiring Job Order personnel whose functions overlap with
those regular employees or are deemed recurring, necessary, and
essential to the Municipality; and
c. implement strict control measures to ensure timely submission of Daily
Time Records (DTRs) before the actual payout date as basis for payroll
preparation.

9.33 Nevertheless, we henceforth recommended that the Local Chief Executive,
thru the MENRO and his subordinates:
(i) enjoin all barangays to support the solid waste management program
of the municipality through continued efforts to educate their
respective constituents on the proper segregation of reusable or
recyclable materials from compostable wastes, strict enforcement of
“No Segregation, No Collection Policy”; and establishment of
functional MRF in all barangays or clustered MRF among barangays;
(ii) tap the services of HOLCIM to provide technical assistance and
training on proper waste segregation and packaging, and information
drive on Safety and Healthy Practices at the MRF, as stipulated in the
MOA;
(iii) strengthen the implementation of existing ordinances and policies on
solid waste management at the barangay level in order to achieve a
community compliance and eventually abide by the provisions on
preserving and protecting the environment required under R.A. No.
9003; and
(iv) coordinate with the Accounting Office, on a monthly basis, the details
of bags of cement for proper recognition and disclosure in the books
of accounts of the municipality.

10.21 We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the:
a) Municipal Planning and Development Officer, together with the GAD
Focal Person and TWG, to exert effort in establishing a GAD database
that will serve as basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and
policy formulation;
b) GAD Focal Point Person to (i) maximize the implementation of GAD
approved/endorsed programs; (ii) consistently coordinate with the MBO
to ensure the accuracy of reports submitted to oversight agencies, and (iii)
to communicate any changes in the GAD Plan and Budget to the DILG; and
c) GAD FPS to (i) prioritize the capacity building of its members by
appropriating funds to attend training and seminars on the GAD; and (ii)
continually comply with the GAD mechanisms and processes to sustain the
LGU’s critical consciousness in supporting gender and development,
women’s empowerment and responding to gender issues pursuant to
PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC Nos. 2013-01 and 2016-01.

11.10 However, for past due accounts based on the GSIS-EBCS, we reiterated our
recommendations that the Local Chief Executive:
a) instruct the Accounting Office to consistently confer with the GSIS until
the discrepancies between the records of the Municipality and the GSIS
are resolved; and
b) require the concerned official and/or employee to settle or restructure
their arrears.

12.6 We commended Management on proper withholding of premiums and loan
repayments of its personnel, in compliance with the provisions of R.A. No. 9679 and
its IRR, and the prompt remittance thereof together with the government shares
within the prescribed period outlined in HDMF Circular No. 275. We also
recommend that the Accounting Office continually observe the requirements of both
issuances.

13.9 We commended Management for the continuous compliance with existing
laws, rules, and regulations on proper deduction and remittance of PhilHealth
contributions of its employees and personnel pursuant to the provisions of R.A. No.
10606, as amended. However, we recommended that the concerned personnel
promptly act on the reconciliation between the records to prevent possible issues
and problems that may be encountered by the insured in availing benefits therefrom.

14.5 We commended Management for the prompt remittance of taxes to the BIR
this year and we recommended continued adherence to withholding and timely
remittance in line with Revenue Memorandum Circular Nos. 23-007 and 23-2012,
and BIR Revenue Regulations No. 2-98.

LDRRM Officer (i) prepare the LDRRMFIP in accordance with COA Circular 2012-002; and (ii) include under a separate caption, the list of projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years as well as the funds from other sources;

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the:
Chairman and Members of the BAC and TWG to (i) carefully scrutinize the documentary requirements submitted by bidder/s and conduct verification thereof to ascertain any commitment by the bidder/s and prevent any conflicting information thereon; and (ii) ensure the submission by the winning bidder of a performance security prior to signing of the contract to safeguard the government from any untoward act of the former, if there be any;

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) consider the aforecited rules and regulations on the hiring of service providers; (b) strictly implement control measures on the required submission of DTRs by service providers prior to actual day of payout as basis in the preparation of payrolls; (c) avoid hiring of service providers whose functions either (i) overlap or duplicate with those of the regular employees or (ii) are considered recurring, necessary, and essential to the Municipality; (d) encourage the service providers to take the Civil Service Exam for them to be qualified and be filled in as regular or permanent employees of the Municipality by providing them free review and payment fee for such exam; and (e) ensure that the service providers, who have reached the compulsory retirement age of 65, are occupationally qualified based on the positions applied for or offered.

through the MENRO and Liga ng mga Barangay, (i) encourage the active participation of barangay officials to support the solid waste management program of the government by educating their respective constituents on the proper segregation of reusable or recyclable materials from compostable wastes and coordinate efforts in the implementation of the five basic environmental laws; and (ii)  strengthen the implementation of existing ordinances and policies on solid waste management at the barangay level in order to achieve a community compliance and eventually abide by the provisions on preserving and protecting the environment required under R.A. No. 9003; and
as far as the Municipal budget permits, provide necessary support in enjoining barangays of the municipality to establish their own or clustered MRF.

lead the Local Development Council in improving the planning and project identification of projects for funding under the 20% Development Fund by referring to the latest guidelines under DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017 – 1 dated February 22, 2017, giving priority to capital investments;  

continue to invest in high-impact capital expenditure projects in the utilization of its 20% Development Fund and refrain from proposing non-capital investment projects/programs under the Fund; and  

c) maintain a special account and subsidiary ledgers for the Development Fund to easily access financial information and improve monitoring on its utilization.
observations.managementAction: The utilization of the 20% DF being followed is the DBM-DaF-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1 dated November 4, 2020.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and heads of concerned offices to implement without further delay those remaining projects not completed/ implemented in CY 2020 and to continue to monitor and supervise the effective and efficient implementation of 2019-2020 projects funded from various source agencies within the timelines of the MOA, as far as feasible.
observations.managementAction:
The utilization of the ₱5,000,000.00 aid from the Provincial Government was completed and fully implemented in 2021.
The DENR-EMB project on the other hand was already completed as of April 14, 2021.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive become more active in the implementation of the Solid Waste Management Act:  

through the MENRO and Liga ng mga Barangay, (i) encourage active participation of barangay officials to support the solid waste management program of the government by educating their respective constituents on the proper segregation of reusable or recyclable materials from compostable wastes and coordinate efforts in the implementation of the five basic environmental laws; and (ii)  strengthen the implementation of existing ordinances and policies on solid waste management at the barangay level in order to achieve a community compliance and eventually abide by the provisions on preserving and protecting the environment required under R.A. No. 9003; and  

b. as far as the Municipal budget permits, provide necessary support in enjoining the barangays to make their MRF fully operational.
observations.managementAction: The municipality and its 15 barangays had a documented agreement regarding segregated waste brought to the municipal MRF.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the:  

MDRRMO and members of the emergency response team (i) attend trainings to gain knowledge and understanding, awareness and skills for prevention, mitigation, preparedness, response, rehabilitation and recovery; and (ii) maintain the minimum standards of power tools/heavy equipment at the minimum as required by the disaster preparedness minimum standards volume 2 of the National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council.
observations.managementAction:
The MDRRMO was instructed to revisit the LDRRM Budget Plans and incorporate thereto the necessary trainings and equipment to ensure that the municipality will be fully disaster-prepared in the next years.
Some payments made were not in accordance with RA 9184 and its Revised IRR, GPPB Circular No. 1-2020 and COA and GPPB Joint Memorandum Circular 01-2020.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and the Municipal HRMO:  

revise the Contract of Service to include a clause stating that the employment of JO personnel is limited to “No work, No pay” basis, instead of monthly basis to avoid undue payment of wages;  

require the submission of their duly verified/accepted Daily Time Records (DTRs) bi-monthly and monthly Accomplishment Report prior to the payment of their salaries; and  

(c) evaluate the performance of personnel and review the necessity of their employment.

observations.recommendation:
We commended the Management for its efforts in the establishment and approval of its CLUP for CY 2016-2025, Comprehensive Development Plan (CDP), and in complying with some of minimum standards of disaster preparedness as required by the National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council.  Likewise, we recommended that the officials concerned continue to accelerate efforts in training its emergency rescue team to be able to be well prepared when disaster strike.

However, for the Municipality to be considered fully disaster-prepared, we recommended that the Management, thru the MDRRM Office, comply with the completion of items listed in the checklist cited in the NDRRMC Disaster Preparedness Minimum Standards, Vol. 2 pursuant to RA 10121.  If the Municipality’s budget may not permit it, request financial assistance from the Provincial Government of Pampanga or other source agencies, as the case may be, for the funding of high-value requirements such as the construction of a permanent evacuation center and disaster-heavy equipment.

observations.recommendation:
We commend management of its efforts to achieve effective planning, implementation and monitoring of the Agency’s GAD PPAs.  However, we recommend that the (a) GAD focal person initiate the establishment and maintenance of the GAD database to serve as basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation; and 
(b) GAD board re-evaluate the achievement of objectives thru the full implementation of programmed GAD PPAs before augmenting the same to other PPAs, and (c) abide by the schedule on GAD Planning and budgeting provided by Joint Memorandum Circular 2012-001.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, Sangguniang Bayan and Local Development Council strictly comply with the Memorandum of Agreement agreed upon by both parties (Funding agencies and the implementing agency) and ensure the effective and efficient implementation of the proposed projects within their targeted completion period through close monitoring and supervision to maximize the developmental benefits and the early enjoyment thereof by the intended beneficiaries.

We commend the LCE and MDRRMO Officer and its staff of their efforts to comply with the provisions of the Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of Republic Act No. 10121, otherwise known as the Philippine Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Act of 2010 and other related rules/regulations/laws regarding disasters preparedness.  However, we would still recommend that the Local Chief Executive require:
 a. the MDRRMO to continue efforts to train, research on and conduct planning to improve further competencies in  operations and warning to effectively and efficiently address the requirements of the existing rules and regulations on disaster preparedness, mitigation, recovery and response;
 b.    as head of the MDC, to fast track the preparation of the Municipality’s Comprehensive Development Plan (CDP) and Comprehensive Land-Use Plan (CLUP) in order that disaster risk management activities could be integrated to the development plan of the Municipality to prevent further loss of lives and properties; and
  
c. Members of the MDRRMC ensure that the member CSOs/NGOs to the Council possess the eligibility requirements cited under NDRRMC No.  3 to assure of effective and strengthened citizen’s participation in the locality.

observations.managementAction:
a.     Improved the participation of the Liga ng mga Barangay in the solid waste management programs of the municipality.


b.     Strengthen the compliance of the barangays in the requirements of waste segregation law.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Assessor to complete the entries in the Assessment Roll with accuracy and submit the required certified list and assessment roll, respectively, to the Municipal Accountant to pave the way for the preparation of accounting entries to recognize the receivable and  liability accounts as prescribed by Section 20 of the Manual on NGAS for the LGUs and Section 248 of the Local Government Code of 1991.

We recommended that the Accounting Office, GSO and Inventory Committee, as the case may be (a) prepare and maintain property ledger cards and schedules for the PPE; (b) issue ARE for all purchased PPE upon issuance to end-user to establish accountability; (c) mobilize additional competent personnel from other departments to facilitate the completion of the physical count of all PPE items and in the event that documents needed to obtain costing of PPE is no longer feasible, consider the fair value thereof as point of reference pursuant to PPSAS 17; (d) strengthen the controls on the handling and recording procedures for the PPE by performing a periodic reconciliation of the records of the accounting, the property and actual inventory results; (e) set up a spreadsheet application or lapsing schedule for the PPE items to facilitate the computation of  the periodic depreciation and recognition for each PPE item; (f) create an appraisal committee for the un-booked parcels of land and record the same under the PPE account; (g) exert efforts to secure the necessary documents and reports so that appropriate depreciation can be provided for the PPE accounts, and (h) require the Municipal Treasury, Supply Officer and the Municipal Assessor to ascertain the status of parcels of land owned by the agency with titling issues and work for the titling thereof in the name of the Municipality of Minalin,  assuming funds have been provided thereof.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer/Property Official and Municipal Assessor (a) file with the GSIS the insurance coverage of all the Municipality’s insurable assets, assuming the funds thereto have already been provided for; and (b) work out for the immediate transfer of the titles of the property which are not yet in the name of the municipality, assuming funds have already been set aside for the purpose.

We commended the LCE and MSWD Officer and its staff of its efforts to uplift the living condition of the poor.  We would still recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the:
Municipal Social Welfare Officer to ensure that the nutritional status of the minimal number of under-nourished children improve to normal and exert more efforts to maintain or improve further the number of children within the normal weight; and
As far as practicable, considering the maintaining balance requirement of the account, Municipal Treasurer to comply with Section 2.4 of DSWD Administrative Order No. 08 Series of 2012 on the opening of a separate bank account for the Program.

We recommended that the Members of the Municipal Local School Board:
prepare a balanced budget with conscious and concerted efforts of observing compliance with the legal and regulatory requirements on SEF budgeting, allocation and utilization as laid down in the latest guidelines under DepEd, DBM & DILG Joint Circular No. 1 s. of 2017 which specifically identified that the repair and construction of classrooms shall be a priority item in the SEF budget over other eligible items; and
request for inputs from the Deped official/School Division Superintendent in the prioritization of SEF projects and expenditures in accordance with the DepEd criteria and with due regard to the provisions of laws, rules and regulations such as the latest  guidelines in DepEd, DBM & DILG Joint Circular No. 1 s. of 2017 .

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, Sangguniang Bayan and Local Development Council strictly comply with DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 dated April 11, 2011 and (a) continue to invest in high-impact capital expenditures projects in the utilization of its 20% Development Fund  and refrain from proposing non-capital investment projects under the Fund;  and (b) ensure the effective and efficient implementation of the proposed projects within their targeted completion period through close monitoring and supervision to maximize the developmental benefits and the early enjoyment thereof by the intended beneficiaries.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the:
Treasury personnel in charge of the accounts to monitor the amount to be automatically retained with the LGU to be  used for the OBO’s programs and projects and the amount to be remitted to BTr representing the DPWH’s  5% share;
Treasurer and the OBO to closely coordinate for the proper implementation of the OBO’s programs and projects using the Trust Fund in accordance with the pertinent law and regulations; and
Municipal Engineer, acting as the OBO, to exercise prudence in the planning and implementation of programs and projects  using the TF and properly observe the guidelines on the preparation of the program of works.

We recommended that the General Services Officer procure, as far as practicable and feasible, directly from the PS-DBM all anticipated common-use supplies, and resort to local suppliers only upon unavailability of supplies needed using the prescribed alternative modes of procurement.

We recommended that the General Services Officer and other officials/offices concerned furnish the Audit Team with copies of contracts/purchase orders and supporting documents and Inspection and Acceptance Reports as required by COA Circular Nos. 2009-001 and 95-006. This is designed to enable the audit team to perform its duties relating to post audit of the transactions, at the most appropriate time and manner without delaying or obstructing the implementation or prosecution of the project.

However, we recommend that the (a) GAD focal person initiate the establishment and maintenance of the GAD database to serve as basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation; and (b) GAD board re-evaluate the achievement of objectives thru the full implementation of programmed GAD PPAs before augmenting the same to other PPAs, and (c) abide by the schedule on GAD Planning and budgeting provided by Joint Memorandum Circular 2012-001.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, Sangguniang Bayan and Local Development Council strictly comply with the Memorandum of Agreement agreed upon by both parties (Funding agencies and the implementing agency) and ensure the effective and efficient implementation of the proposed projects within their targeted completion period through close monitoring and supervision to maximize the developmental benefits and the early enjoyment thereof by the intended beneficiaries.

However, we would still recommend that the Local Chief Executive require:
a. 	the MDRRMO to continue efforts to train, research on and conduct planning to improve further competencies in  operations and warning to effectively and efficiently address the requirements of the existing rules and regulations on disaster preparedness, mitigation, recovery and response;
b.    as head of the MDC, to fast track the preparation of the Municipality’s Comprehensive Development Plan (CDP) and Comprehensive Land-Use Plan (CLUP) in order that disaster risk management activities could be integrated to the development plan of the Municipality to prevent further loss of lives and properties; and
Members of the MDRRMC ensure that the member CSOs/NGOs to the Council possess the eligibility requirements cited under NDRRMC No.  	3 to assure of effective and strengthened citizen’s participation in the locality.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) through the Liga ng mga Barangay, encourage active participation of barangay officials to support the solid waste management program of the government by educating their respective constituents on the proper segregation of reusable or recyclable materials from compostable wastes; (b) through the ESMO, comply with the requirements of the law on waste segregation by placing trash bins with proper labels and by collecting only those garbage that are properly segregated; (c) ensure that all barangays had existing and operational MRF; (d) recommend to the Sangguniang Bayan (SB) policies and possible measures that will ensure full compliance by the barangays on the provisions of R.A. No. 9003; (e) initiate the implementation of its Anti-Littering policy ordinances as provided by Section 48 of R.A. No. 9003; (f) completely filled up the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board with officials who could actively perform their respective duties and perform their functions as intended; (g) review every two years or as the need arises the Municipal Solid Waste  Management Plan for the purposes of ensuring its sustainability, viability, effectiveness and relevance in relation to local and international developments in the field of solid waste management (h) provide support to the concerned barangay for the construction of secured fence for the MRF for security, health and sanitation purposes, and  (i) implement  their  plan of issuing Citation Tickets in CY 2017, for violator of the Municipal Environmental Code to strengthens their drive for a more aggressive solid waste management program.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Treasurer to 

improve further the frequency of depositing collections and refrain from retaining amounts when making deposits in accordance with   Section 32 of the Manual on New Government Accounting System; and

establish a petty cash fund to handle non-recurring, emergency and petty expenses of the Municipality in compliance with Section 48 of the Manual on New Government Accounting System.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and the MENRO (a) seek approval of the plan from the National Solid Waste Management Commission; (b) coordinate efforts and implement the five basic environmental laws on preserving and protecting the environment required under R.A. No. 9003; (c)involve closely the Barangay officials and civic leaders in the strict implementation on the existing environmental laws and intensify the information campaign at the household level on the ordinances issued by the Municipality; (d) seek the help of private companies/organizations in the conduct of a more aggressive and intensive information campaign on the proper segregation of waste and “no littering” policies; (e) formulate solid waste management programs that would encourage and motivate the public to give their cooperation and full support.
